g.data-narration: Welcome to our lecture on Application Programming Interface - the bridge between software and operating systems.
An API is a set of pre-defined functions that allows applications to communicate with the operating system resources.
g.text: An Application Programming Interface (API) is a standardized set of
functions, protocols, and tools that enable software applications to
communicate with the OS without understanding internal complexities.

g.data-narration: APIs provide crucial benefits: hardware independence, security enforcement, and standardization across software projects.
The API architecture shows how requests flow from applications through the API layer down to the kernel.
Operating systems provide different API types for process control, file management, device management, memory, and networking.
APIs are implemented through system call wrappers, library functions, header files, and the Application Binary Interface.
The API call workflow involves parameter validation, software interrupt, kernel mode transition, execution, and result return.
This POSIX example demonstrates file creation and writing using open(), write(), and close() system call wrappers.
Windows CreateProcess API demonstrates process creation with STARTUPINFO and PROCESS_INFORMATION structures.
POSIX defines portable standards for UNIX-like systems including file, process, thread, and interprocess communication APIs.
g.text: Portable Operating System Interface - defines standardized API for UNIX-like systems

g.data-narration: Windows WIN32 API provides object-based handles, asynchronous I/O, and COM integration for Windows applications.
g.text: Microsoft's core API for Windows development featuring object-based handles

g.data-narration: Understanding the difference: APIs are programming interfaces at user level while system calls are kernel-level operations.
API design requires careful consideration of performance overhead, which includes user-kernel transition and security checks.
APIs power real-world applications including device drivers, cloud computing, mobile OS, and security systems.
In summary, APIs provide essential abstraction, portability, and security for operating system interactions.
g.text: • APIs abstract OS complexity from developers
• POSIX and WIN32 are major API standards
• System calls bridge user and kernel modes

g.data-narration: Remember: APIs are user-level interfaces while system calls are kernel-level. Focus on POSIX functions and security aspects.
g.text: • API ≠ System Call: APIs wrap multiple system calls
• Memorize key POSIX APIs: open(), read(), write(), fork(), exec()
• APIs implement security through parameter validation
• Understand error handling: return -1, check errno
